name: Margarita
cultural_notes: The classic tequila cocktail, typically made with tequila, lime juice, and orange liqueur. Served with salt rim.
safety_notes: Sugar and citrus mask the strength of the tequila
Salt rim can increase thirst, leading to faster drinking
recommendations: Sip slowly; the ice melt will dilute the high ABV over time
Order a side of water to balance the salt intake

name: Martini
cultural_notes: Made with gin and dry vermouth. James Bond's 'shaken, not stirred' popularized it.
safety_notes: Almost entirely comprised of high-proof spirits
Very low dilution compared to highballs
risk_factors: Rapid impairment on an empty stomach
recommendations: Limit to one if you are in a professional or solo setting
Always eat a high-protein appetizer before ordering

name: Old Fashioned
cultural_notes: One of the oldest known cocktails, made by muddling sugar with bitters and adding whiskey.
safety_notes: One of the highest ABV cocktails served in a standard glass
Intense flavor usually encourages slow sipping
recommendations: Let the ice block melt slightly to open up the flavors and lower the ABV
Stick to one per hour

name: Negroni
cultural_notes: Equal parts gin, Campari, and sweet vermouth. Created in Florence in 1919.
safety_notes: The bitterness of Campari can mask the alcohol content
Usually served over ice which helps with slow dilution
risk_factors: Herbal ingredients may cause stomach sensitivity for some
recommendations: Pair with snacks (aperitivo style) like olives or nuts to buffer the alcohol

name: Pisco Sour
cultural_notes: National drink of Peru and Chile, made with pisco, lime, and egg white.
safety_notes: Egg white creates a smooth texture that makes it very easy to drink quickly
Citrus acidity is high
risk_factors: Rapid consumption due to smooth texture
recommendations: Ask for a fresh-cracked egg version rather than a pre-mix
Treat as a single-glass experience due to acidity

name: Piscola
cultural_notes: Chile's national drink - pisco mixed with cola.
safety_notes: Cola provides a caffeine/sugar kick that can mask feelings of intoxication
Very easy to drink in large quantities
recommendations: Monitor total volume; highballs go down faster than spirit-forward drinks

name: Caipirinha
cultural_notes: Brazil's national cocktail, made with cachaça, lime, and sugar.
safety_notes: Usually very strong at the bottom of the glass where the sugar and spirit settle
Refreshing taste is deceptive for its 20% ABV
risk_factors: Sun exposure while drinking on beaches
recommendations: Stir frequently to distribute the spirit and sugar
Drink water between rounds if in the sun

name: Chuflay
cultural_notes: Bolivia's signature cocktail - singani with ginger ale and lime.
safety_notes: Standard highball safety profile
Ginger ale can soothe the stomach but masks the spirit well
recommendations: A good 'entry-level' cocktail for those exploring South American spirits

name: The Caesar
cultural_notes: Canada's national cocktail, similar to a Bloody Mary but made with Clamato (clam-tomato juice). Created in Calgary in 1969.
manufacturing_notes: Garnished with celery, often with bacon or shrimp
safety_notes: Low ABV but high sodium content
Often contains shellfish allergens (clam juice)
risk_factors: High sodium leads to water retention
recommendations: Always disclose shellfish allergies
Drink water alongside to balance the high salt intake

name: Highball
cultural_notes: In Japan, specifically whiskey and soda. A cultural phenomenon that revolutionized whiskey drinking. Served in tall glasses with lots of ice.
manufacturing_notes: Proper ice and stirring technique crucial
safety_notes: High dilution makes it easier to track intake
Carbonation increases alcohol absorption speed
risk_factors: Deceptive drinkability due to light flavor
recommendations: Excellent for long dinner sessions
Stick to one per 45-60 minutes to maintain pace

name: Chu-hi
cultural_notes: Short for shochu highball. Shochu mixed with soda water and fruit flavor. Extremely popular, available canned everywhere.
safety_notes: Often sold in 'Strong' versions (9%+) which significantly increase risk
Fruit flavors mask the taste of shochu
risk_factors: High sugar in commercial canned versions
recommendations: Check the can label for ABV, as 'Strong' versions vary significantly
Great for casual sipping with street food

name: Spritz
cultural_notes: The quintessential Italian aperitivo cocktail. Typically Aperol, prosecco, and soda water. Served with olives or chips.
safety_notes: Lower ABV than most cocktails
Bubbly nature can cause faster initial absorption
risk_factors: High sugar content from the liqueur
Dehydration if consumed in direct sun
recommendations: Best consumed with the provided salty snacks to pace the alcohol
Ideal for early evening social starts

name: RTD Cocktails
cultural_notes: Ready-to-drink canned cocktails. Massive growth globally as consumers seek convenience and quality.
safety_notes: Consistent ABV makes tracking easy
Portability can lead to drinking in settings without water or food access
risk_factors: Fast pace of consumption due to convenience
recommendations: Keep an eye on the total number of cans
Always dispose of cans responsibly in outdoor settings

name: Hard Seltzers
cultural_notes: Sparkling water with alcohol and fruit flavor. Exploded in popularity as a low-calorie, gluten-free alternative to beer.
safety_notes: Very low calorie and sugar, reducing typical 'heavy' feelings
Clear liquid appearance can lead to confusion with water
risk_factors: Very easy to drink multiple in short succession
recommendations: Excellent choice for long daytime events
Alternate with plain sparkling water to stay hydrated
